name: The Highland Center Corporation
description_short: The Highland Center is a Shreveport-based non-profit community hub offering free HUD-certified housing counseling, VITA tax prep, financial literacy, and emergency support services to low-income residents.
description_long:
The Highland Center Corporation is a community-based non-profit organization located in Shreveport, Louisiana, serving as a multi-service hub for residents facing financial hardship and housing instability. Founded on principles of community support and mutual aid, the organization operates from its downtown location at 520 Olive Street and partners with multiple local and national organizations to deliver comprehensive services.

The Center's financial assistance programs include free HUD-certified housing counseling (covering homebuying, renting, credit, and budgeting), Volunteer Income Tax Assistance (VITA) for households earning under $64,000 annually, hand-up loans coordinated through Citizens Bank & Trust as a payday alternative, and free computer access for financial management. They also provide basic financial infrastructure services like low-fee ATM access ($0.75 withdrawal fee) and mobile fresh produce markets at affordable rates to address food security alongside financial stability.

What distinguishes Highland Center is its integrated approach to poverty alleviation rather than single-issue intervention. Services span immediate needs (meals, clothing, laundry vouchers) alongside wealth-building programs (homebuyer workshops, credit counseling, tax prep). The organization actively engages faith-based partnerships and community volunteers, hosting regular fellowship meals and leveraging donated resources through a thrift store. Their facility also serves as affordable office space for mission-driven organizations, creating economic activity within the community they serve.

The organization appears genuinely non-profit and mission-focused, though their website lacks detailed outcome data, staff credentials beyond "HUD-certified," or independent accreditation information. While all services listed appear free or low-cost and legitimate, potential clients should verify current program availability and registration requirements by phone, as the website lacks detailed eligibility criteria or enrollment processes.
pros: All primary financial services (housing counseling, tax prep, computer training) are completely free with no hidden fees
HUD-certified housing counselor provides one-on-one financial and credit counseling at no cost
VITA program serves households up to $64,000 income annually—broader than many community tax prep programs
Hand-up loans through Citizens Bank & Trust offered as explicit payday loan alternative with lower interest rates
Integrated services model addresses root causes: meals, clothing, laundry, and library access support financial stability
Low-fee ATM ($0.75) reduces banking friction for unbanked/underbanked residents
Faith-based partnership model ensures volunteer capacity and donated resources for sustainability
cons: Website provides no APR rates, terms, or repayment schedules for hand-up loans—critical details missing
No information on housing counseling wait times, appointment availability, or how to register in advance despite 'must register' requirement
Tax prep (VITA) limited to January-April; off-season support unavailable
Computer help hour phone number listed (951) 227-0123 appears to be outside Shreveport area code (318), raising contact reliability concerns
No outcome metrics, client testimonials, or independent accreditation (NFCC, HUD) verification displayed on website
best_for: First-time homebuyers in Shreveport earning under $60K seeking free counseling and monthly workshops
Low-income tax filers (under $64K household income) needing free, IRS-certified tax preparation during tax season
Individuals seeking payday loan alternatives or low-interest emergency loans coordinated through banking partnerships
Unbanked/underbanked residents needing affordable financial infrastructure (ATM access, computer training, bill payment support)
diagnosis: The Highland Center is best suited for low-income Shreveport residents seeking free, legitimate financial counseling and tax prep from a community-based non-profit. The main caveat is that critical loan details (APR, terms, repayment) are absent from their website, requiring direct phone contact, and several services have seasonal or limited availability that must be verified before relying on them.
services: HUD-certified housing counseling (one-on-one, free, covers homebuying, credit, budgeting, renting, homelessness)
First-time homebuyer workshops ($20/person, one Saturday monthly, 9am-3pm)
VITA free tax preparation (January-April, households under $64,000 income)
Hand-up loans (low-interest payday alternative coordinated through Citizens Bank & Trust)
Community library with 1,000+ books, desktop computers, printing, adult reading classes
Free computer help hour and training (Project I/O partnership, requires advance registration)
Highland Blessing dinner (free family meal, Thursdays 5:30pm)
Mobile Market fresh produce sales (third Tuesdays, 12pm, Shreveport Green partnership)
typical_results_timeline: Counseling available within 1-2 weeks of contact.
